summary: Gruber shares an AppleScript he wrote to add a quit confirmation dialog to Safari on macOS, solving the common problem of accidentally quitting the browser when trying to close a tab with ⌘W. He explains how Chrome's press-and-hold ⌘Q solution works but criticizes it as unidiomatic for the Mac. His script counts open windows and tabs, presents a standard macOS confirmation alert, and is triggered via FastScripts which intercepts the ⌘Q shortcut before Safari sees it. He also notes the simpler option of remapping ⌘Q in System Preferences but explains why a confirmation dialog better serves his needs.
key_points: Accidentally quitting a browser by pressing ⌘Q instead of ⌘W is a common annoyance, even with session restoration, because tabs can lose state
Chrome's press-and-hold ⌘Q solution works but is unidiomatic on macOS — confirmation dialogs are the traditional Mac approach to preventing data loss
A simple AppleScript combined with FastScripts can intercept ⌘Q and show a proper quit confirmation alert
System Preferences allows remapping any menu shortcut in any app, which Gruber calls one of the best power-user tips, but it doesn't provide the confirmation behavior he wants
money_quotes: Quitting Safari and closing tabs are completely discrete, and it's clear to me that's the correct design.
Press-and-hold to invoke a menu key shortcut just isn't a thing on the Mac. It's weird.
That's not even a good description for the setting. You don't get warned before quitting when it's enabled — you're instead required to press-and-hold the ⌘Q shortcut.
But a confirmation warning is exactly what you should get. This is how the Mac has protected against quitting when you might lose data or state since the dawn of time.
Now I can wildly stab at ⌘W to close tabs without a care in the world.
This ability to fully customize every menu key shortcut in every single app on the system is one of the best power-user tips I know of.
key_insight: Chrome's press-and-hold quit mechanism violates Mac conventions; the proper macOS solution is a confirmation dialog, which can be implemented with a simple AppleScript.
